common_name: Narrow-Leaf Bird of Paradise (Rushleaf Bird of Paradise)

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; narrow-leaf bird of paradise is slower to establish and flower than S. reginae and more drought-tolerant once established. It takes 3–5 years to flower from division. Requires protection below 28°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; narrow-leaf bird of paradise is a remarkable South African species distinct from the familiar Bird of Paradise (S. reginae) in a unique way — it lacks leaf blades entirely. Instead of the large, paddle-shaped leaves of S. reginae, S. juncea produces erect, cylindrical, rush-like petioles (leaf stalks without a blade) forming a bold clump of upright, gray-green cylindrical stems that gives the plant a dramatic, minimalist, architectural silhouette resembling a cluster of giant rush or bamboo. From within this distinctive foliage emerge the same striking orange-and-cobalt-blue bird of paradise flowers as the common species, appearing on stiff stalks from spring through summer. More drought-tolerant than S. reginae because the reduced leaf surface area minimizes water loss — an elegant adaptation to hot, dry habitats in the South African Eastern Cape. Outstanding as an architectural statement plant in drought-tolerant, Mediterranean-climate, and sub-tropical gardens where its unusual leafless form creates immediate visual interest.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; Chilean glory flower grows rapidly from seed and begins blooming within 3–4 months. In zones 8–10 it is perennial above or below ground; in colder zones grow as an annual. It climbs by tendrils to 8–15 feet and requires a trellis, fence, or shrub to support it.

directions: Start seeds indoors early and train on a trellis in full sun; Chilean glory flower is a vigorous tendril-climbing vine from Chile and Peru producing masses of small (1 inch), tubular flowers in brilliant orange-red to scarlet (with yellow and orange-yellow forms also available) in long racemes from summer through the first hard frost — an extended, prolific bloom display on a fast-growing vine that reaches 8–15 feet. The small, bright flowers resemble a more refined version of Eccremocarpus, with the tubular shape irresistible to hummingbirds during migration. It climbs using leaf tendrils over trellises, fences, shrubs, and netting with remarkable speed. In zones 8–10 the roots may survive winter to resprout; in colder climates treat as an annual started from seed each spring. A good combination vine with purple-flowered plants such as Cobaea scandens or morning glory, where the orange-red flowers create striking complementary color.

planting.note: Plant small corms 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full sun; rhodohypoxis requires moist conditions during the growing season (spring through fall) but must be kept completely dry in winter when dormant, as wet-cold conditions cause rotting. In wetter climates, grow in containers that can be kept dry in winter or lift corms for frost-free dry storage.

directions: Plant small corms in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun; rose grass is a jewel-like South African alpine perennial from the Drakensberg mountains producing diminutive tufts of narrow, hairy, grass-like leaves (hence 'rose grass') only 2–4 inches tall, completely smothered in bright, cheerful, six-petaled flowers throughout summer from May to September. The flowers — individually only half an inch across but produced in remarkable abundance relative to the plant's tiny size — are in rich shades of deep rose-pink, pale pink, or white depending on the cultivar. The resulting effect is a dense carpet of concentrated, happy color disproportionate to the miniature plant size, making it exceptional for rock gardens, alpine troughs, container gardens, and front-of-border edging. It requires the unusual combination of moist summer growing conditions and completely dry winter dormancy — easy to provide in a pot, more challenging in open ground in wet climates.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or divide established clumps in spring; Serbian bellflower spreads vigorously by surface runners and can become invasive in small rock gardens or between paving stones. It self-sows modestly. Shear back hard after the main June–July flush to encourage a repeat bloom in late summer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to part shade; Serbian bellflower is one of the most prolific and cheerful bellflowers — a low, trailing, vigorously spreading perennial from the Dalmatian limestone mountains producing a relentless flood of small, star-shaped, lavender-blue to lilac flowers from June through August (with a possible rebloom in fall if cut back), carried on trailing stems 6–8 inches tall that spread outward over rocks, walls, and pavement in a cheerful scrambling cascade. The flowers, though individually simple and small, are produced in such extraordinary abundance that a mature clump is a solid color mass for weeks. Outstanding for trailing over walls, filling cracks between stone, scrambling down banks, and naturalizing in difficult dry positions in part shade. It spreads more vigorously than most bellflowers and can colonize large areas over time. The cultivar 'Stella' has a somewhat more compact habit than the species.

planting.note: Sow on the soil surface in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun to part shade; wall bellflower is a natural crevice plant and thrives in gaps between paving stones, in dry stone walls, and in rock garden crevices where perfect drainage around the crown prevents winter rotting. Less spreading than C. poscharskyana.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; wall bellflower is a compact, neat, semi-evergreen perennial from rocky cliff faces of Dalmatia, Croatia, producing masses of rich, deep blue-purple, bell-shaped flowers in June and July above a carpet of small, dark-green, heart-shaped, slightly toothed leaves. More compact and more neatly behaved than its relative C. poscharskyana, it is particularly outstanding for planting in the crevices of dry stone walls and between paving stones, where its natural cliff-dwelling habit is replicated and perfect drainage around the crown prevents the crown rot that afflicts it in heavy soils. The dark, saturated blue-purple of the flowers is notably rich compared to the lighter lavender of C. poscharskyana. Semi-evergreen to evergreen in mild winters. One of the standard reliable perennials for rock gardens, wall crevices, and alpine troughs, performing best in thin, lean soils where better-fed companions would outcompete it.
